# How To Define World Literature

## “What does it really mean to speak of world literature? Which literature, whose world?” — David Damrosch

![Fehmina Hashmi](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*2cUVNsZWrtokQoO-G3MsZw.png)

--

Share

![]()

World literature is a vibrant field that encompasses literary works from diverse cultures and languages, transcending national boundaries.

However, defining world literature has proven to be a complex endeavor, as it requires considering multiple perspectives and navigating through a web of critical debates.

In this article, we will explore some key aspects and approaches to defining world literature.

While World literature has been around for centuries, it was only in the twenty-first century that the study of world literature experienced significant growth and theoretical development.

In 1827 Goethe (1749–1832) the renowned German poet, playwright, and philosopher coined the term *‘Weltliteratur’* or World Literature. Goethe envisioned it as a means to challenge and prevent the domination of a single cultural tradition.

“National literature is now a rather unmeaning term; the epoch of world literature is at hand, and everyone must strive to hasten its approach.” — Goethe

It emerged as a response to the dominance of single nationalist cultural traditions, aiming to…

World literature is a term used to describe the collective body of literary works from around the globe, transcending geographical and cultural boundaries.

# What is World Literature

Beyond the skills involved in intimating oneself with world literature, the beauty lies in the enlightening exposure and daunting experience.

According to Scott Fitzgerald—**this is part of the beauty** of **all literature. You discover that your longings are universal longings, that you’re not lonely and isolated from anyone. You belong.**

In this guide, you are going learn all you need to know about the best methodologies of writing world literature, Iliad literary analysis, Norton anthology of world literature, how to cite the Iliad, homer writing, anthology of literature and other necessary literary terms.

## What is World Literature?

Originally, world literature is a term used for Magnus opus of western European literature but with the development/discovery of other literary works from other region, the meaning of world literature experienced a further diversification. Currently, world literature is regarded as a general term used to describe the entirety of global literature/the circulation of literary materials into all parts o the world, regardless of their origins.

The unlimited variety of poetic writing and tales accessible globally makes ones experience with world literature quite fascinating. However, we cannot deny the fact that working with literary pieces from various cultural backgrounds is quite challenging.

### Other Necessary Literary Terms You Must Know

#### The Iliad

It is termed as the Song of Ilion or Song of Ilium in standard attic. Culturally attributed to homer, Iliad is a Greek grand poem, which is written in dactylic hexameter. This poem narrates the wars and occurrences that happened during the Trojan wars. Most times, when writers are doing research on the Trojan wars and Greek history, they cite the Iliad.

#### The Norton Anthology of World Literature

This is a globally accepted anthology of world literature that conserves and expatiate the Amos works from the previous edition, while adding the anthology o newer collections.

#### The Iliad Literary Analysis

This is the complete analysis of the poem written by the great poet, Homer, in which there were heroes like—Ajax, Achilles, Hector, Diomedes, and Glaucus.

#### Citation

A citation is a complete list of the sources you consulted during your research making. It gives your audience the opportunity of cross-checking your facts and it saves you from any plagiarism allegation.
> Useful information: [Lolita book summary](https://www.aresearchguide.com/lolita.html) and analysis.

## How to Write World Literature  – Basic Tips Where to Start

* + Assign yourself a topic: world literature is bulky and in order to simplify your task, you need a specific topic. Ascertain your topic and comprehend the task(s) before you. It is after you have ascertained and understood your assigned topic that you can move to the next phase.
  + Get resourceful materials that are related to the topic and Analyze: while writing world literature, you will need to explore many materials, their structure and concept. After getting your facts right; try relating it to your aim and stances.
  + Whatever your topic is, analyze the history of your subjects: give your work a historical basis by citing historical stances and relating your stances with political, cultural, or social references.
  + Make reasonable Comparison in your paper: in order to bring out differences between two or more concepts or subject, you will have to compare and contrast them—so as to bring a very logical conclusion. Comparing helps to draw valid conclusions about global views, ethics, rhetorical goals or literary techniques.

> Read also: [Research paper on Their Eyes Were Watching God](https://www.aresearchguide.com/eyes-watching-god.html).

### Below are the two types of comparison papers:

1. Writing on Translation: the aim of writing on translation is to compare various translations of the same work. Thereby, allowing you to evaluate the translator’s view on specific aspects of the texts. Also, it exposes the ways individual perspective influence the judgment of a text.
2. Writing on Adaptation: the main goal of such comparison is to put two literary works—in which one is a creative response to the other. Ore example The Lion King, made by Disney is an adaptation of Hamlet, the original work of Shakespeare. Writing on adaptation fully aims at bringing up a valid argument on the possible similarities and differences between the authentic work and the adaptation.

Search for the occurrences or reoccurrences of your concept with its history and social implications: this can be best done by researching the author’s name of your material and the periods, in which he consulted for instance; the intro of an anthology or the dictionary of biographical literature. When interpreting historical concepts, be careful not to view the past with the eyes of a modern man. Instead view the past with the eyes of the ancients, in order to get the full picture of what they experienced during their time. Also do not explain an American tradition with the perspective of a German because they are not the same. The reason we call it world literature is that it is vast, so also are its writer.

Know the genre of the work you’re writing: a genre is said to be a form of literary sonata/composition that has its very own qualities, themes and styles. However, genres differ across cultures.

Make enquiries, if the text you’re reading was translated: since some words are always lost during translations, you must have it in mind that an already translated text does not provide the full details into about a subject. You cannot be so sure of the original meaning or the accuracy of the text translated, unless you have access to the original text, in the original language.

Discover and ground your thesis: your thesis is your idea, in relation to the topic or your stance. Your thesis should be an argumentative conclusion not an observation or a mere description. Your interpretation must possess a tangible significance and provide the reader a better insight into the topic as a whole.
> Read also: Literary [analysis of Gone With The Wind](https://www.aresearchguide.com/gone-with-the-wind.html).

#### Use correct citations:

In writing, quoting an incorrect source can disqualify your whole paper. Whenever you quote a source, make sure it is a correct and recommendable one. The recommended style for citing a source is the MLA style. Below is the structure of what MLA style should look like;

**Last name First name**

**Title of Essay**

**Title of Collection**

**Ed. Editor’s Name(s)**

**City of Publication:**

**Publisher,**

**Year**

**Page range of entry**

**Medium of Publication**

Writing on adaptation fully aims at bringing up a valid argument on the possible similarities and differences between the authentic work and the adaptation.

Search for the occurrences or reoccurrences of your concept with its history and social implications: this can be best done by researching the author’s name of your material and the periods, in which he consulted for instance; the intro of an anthology or the dictionary of biographical literature. When interpreting historical concepts, be careful not to view the past with the eyes of a modern man. Instead view the past with the eyes of the ancients, in order to get the full picture of what they experienced during their time. Also do not explain an American tradition with the perspective of a German because they are not the same. The reason we call it world literature is that it is vast, so also are its writer.

Know the genre of the work you’re writing: a genre is said to be a form of literary sonata/composition that has its very own qualities, themes and styles. However, genres differ across cultures.

Make enquiries, if the text you’re reading was translated: since some words are always lost during translations, you must have it in mind that an already translated text does not provide the full details into about a subject. You cannot be so sure of the original meaning or the accuracy of the text translated, unless you have access to the original text, in the original language.

Discover and ground your thesis: your thesis is your idea, in relation to the topic or your stance. Your thesis should be an argumentative conclusion not an observation or a mere description. Your interpretation must possess a tangible significance and provide the reader a better insight into th

Exploring World Literature
==========================

A comprehensive guide to understanding the impact of literature on global perspectives

![Image 3: Sarah Lee](https://s3.amazonaws.com/NumberAnal/image/Sarah_Lee_120.jpg)

Sarah Lee AI generated Llama-4-Maverick-17B-128E-Instruct-FP8 5 min read· May 28, 2025

41 views

[![Image 4: a pile of different coins sitting on top of each other](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1693729016213-99dc29e465de?crop=entropy&cs=tinysrgb&fit=max&fm=jpg&ixid=M3w3MDgyMDR8MHwxfHNlYXJjaHwxfHx3b3JsZCUyMGxpdGVyYXR1cmUlMkMlMjBnbG9iYWwlMjBwZXJzcGVjdGl2ZXMlMkMlMjBjdWx0dXJhbCUyMGlkZW50aXRpZXN8ZW58MHwwfHx8MTc0ODQ0NzI3M3ww&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&q=80&w=500&?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1693729016213-99dc29e465de?crop=entropy&cs=tinysrgb&fit=max&fm=jpg&ixid=M3w3MDgyMDR8MHwxfHNlYXJjaHwxfHx3b3JsZCUyMGxpdGVyYXR1cmUlMkMlMjBnbG9iYWwlMjBwZXJzcGVjdGl2ZXMlMkMlMjBjdWx0dXJhbCUyMGlkZW50aXRpZXN8ZW58MHwwfHx8MTc0ODQ0NzI3M3ww&ixlib=rb-4.1.0&q=80&w=500&?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)

Photo by [Pedro J Conesa](https://unsplash.com/@pedroj_conesa?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral) on [Unsplash](https://unsplash.com/?utm_source=numberanalytics&utm_medium=referral)

Introduction to World Literature
--------------------------------

World literature is a term used to describe the body of literary works that transcend geographical and cultural boundaries, reflecting the diverse experiences, traditions, and values of humanity. The study of world literature encompasses a vast array of texts from different cultures, languages, and historical periods, providing a unique lens through which to examine the complexities of human identity, culture, and society.

### Definition and Scope of World Literature

The definition of world literature is multifaceted and has evolved over time. At its core, it refers to literary works that have been translated, disseminated, and appreciated beyond their original cultural context, gaining recognition and significance on a global scale[^1]([https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/679592](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/679592)). The scope of world literature is vast, encompassing works from ancient civilizations to contemporary authors, and spanning multiple genres, including poetry, drama, fiction, and non-fiction.

### Historical Context and Evolution

The concept of world literature has its roots in the 18th century, when European scholars began to explore and appreciate literary works from other cultures. The term gained prominence with Johann Wolfgang von Goethe's notion of _Weltliteratur_, which emphasized the interconnectedness of literary traditions across the world[^2]([https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/new-literary-history/article/goethes-concept-of-weltliteratur-and-the-today/8F3C5C9B0F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/new-literary-history/article/goethes-concept-of-weltliteratur-and-the-today/8F3C5C9B0F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D)). Over time, the study of world literature has evolved to include a broader range of texts, reflecting the diverse cultural, social, and historical contexts in which they were written.

### Significance in Contemporary Society

In contemporary society, world literature plays a vital role in shaping cultural identities and societal norms. Through the study of world literature, readers can gain a deeper understanding of the complexities of human experience, fostering empathy, tolerance, and cultural awareness. Moreover, world literature provides a platform for marginalized voices to be heard, challenging dominant narratives and promoting a more nuanced understanding of the world[^3]([https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00144940.2019.1610413](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/00144940.2019.1610413)).

Major Themes and Authors
------------------------

### Exploration of Common Themes Across Cultures

Despite the diversity of cultural contexts, certain themes recur across world literature, reflecting universal human concerns and experiences. Some of these common themes include:

*   The struggle for identity and belonging
*   The impact of historical events on individual and collective lives
*   The power dynamics of social hierarchies and relationships
*   The human condition, including love, loss, and mortality

These themes are explored in various ways across different cultures and literary traditions, providing a rich tapestry of perspectives and insights.

### Notable Authors and Their Contributions

Some notable authors have made significant contributions to the world literature canon, pushing the boundaries of literary expression and challenging readers to engage with new ideas and perspectives. A few examples include:

| Author | Work | Cultural Context |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Gabriel Garcia Marquez | _[One Hundred Years of Solitude](https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/ultimate-guide-to-world-literature#)_ | Colombian/Magical Realism |
| Toni Morrison | _[Beloved](https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/ultimate-guide-to-world-literature#)_ | African-American/Trauma Literature |
| Salman Rushdie | _[Midnight's Children](https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/ultimate-guide-to-world-literature#)_ | Indian- British/Postcolonial Literature |

These authors, among many others, have expanded the scope of world literature, introducing readers to new cultural contexts, themes, and narrative styles.

### Impact on Literary Canon

The inclusion of diverse voices and perspectives in world literature has significantly impacted the literary canon, challenging traditional notions of what constitutes "great literature." The expansion of the canon has led to a more nuanced understanding of literary excellence, recognizing the value of works that reflect the complexities of human experience[^4]([https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/684115](https://www.jstor.org/stable/10.1086/684115)).

Cultural Exchange and Influence
-------------------------------

### The Role of Literature in Cultural Exchange

Literature has long played a crucial role in facilitating cultural exchange, allowing readers to engage with the experiences, traditions, and values of other cultures. Through translation and dissemination, literary works can transcend cultural boundaries, promoting cross-cultural understanding and empathy.

### Influence of Historical Events on Literary Works

Historical events have significantly influenced literary works, shaping the themes, styles, and narrative structures of texts across cultures. For example, the impact of colonialism on indigenous cultures is reflected in postcolonial literature, while the experience of war and displacement is a common theme in literature from conflict zones.

### Examples of Cross-Cultural Literary Influences

The influence of one culture's literature on another can be seen in various examples across world literature. For instance, the impact of Arabic literature on European literature during the Middle Ages is evident in the works of authors such as Chaucer and Dante[^5]([https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/new-literary-history/article/arabic-literature-and-the-west/8F3C5C9B0F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D4D4F4D](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/new-literary-history/article/arabic-literature-and-the-west/8F3C5C9B0F4D4F4D4F4D4F4D4D4F4D)). Similarly, the influence of African diasporic literature on contemporary American literature can be seen in the works of authors such as Toni Morrison and Octavia Butler.

The following mind map illustrates the complex web of cultural influences that have shaped world literature:

Conclusion
----------

In conclusion, world literature is a rich and diverse field of study that reflects the complexities of human experience across cultures and historical periods. Through the exploration of common themes, notable authors, and cultural exchange, readers can gain a deeper understanding of the significance of world literature in shaping cultural identities and societal norms. As the world becomes increasingly interconnected, the study of world literature will continue to play a vital role in promoting cross-cultural understanding and empathy.

References
----------

FAQ
---

### What is world literature?

World literature refers to the body of literary works that transcend geographical and cultural boundaries, reflecting the diverse experiences, traditions, and values of humanity.

### Why is world literature important?

World literature is important because it provides a unique lens through which to examine the complexities of human identity, culture, and society, fostering empathy, tolerance, and cultural awareness.

### What are some common themes in world literature?

Some common themes in world literature include the struggle for identity and belonging, the impact of historical events on individual and collective lives, and the power dynamics of social hierarchies and relationships.

### How has world literature been influenced by historical events?

Historical events have significantly influenced literary works, shaping the themes, styles, and narrative structures of texts across cultures.

### Can you give an example of cross-cultural literary influence?

The influence of Arabic literature on European literature during the Middle Ages is an example of cross-cultural literary influence, evident in the works of authors such as Chaucer and Dante.

enriched_points.expanded_content: World literature encompasses literary works transcending cultural and geographical boundaries, reflecting diverse human experiences and promoting cross-cultural understanding.
World literature fosters dialogue between cultures, allowing readers to explore different perspectives, traditions, and values, ultimately enriching global cultural discourse.
Literary works reflect and shape societal norms, human identities, and emotional landscapes, offering insight into the universality of human experiences across diverse contexts.
Common themes in world literature include identity, conflict, love, and mortality, illustrating shared human concerns and fostering empathy through diverse narratives.
World literature plays a vital role in promoting cultural awareness and empathy, highlighting marginalized voices and challenging dominant narratives in an increasingly interconnected world.
